data.title: Zeta forecasts $1.785B 2026 revenue and $235M free cash flow as Athena expands enterprise adoption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zeta Global (ZETA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered our 19th consecutive beat and raise quarter" and said "AI is no longer a feature" and is "driving a replacement cycle" as enterprises demand "fewer systems, measurable results and applied intelligence that works today" (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "First quarter revenue of $396 million" and "adjusted EBITDA was $66 million" were paired with the view that "Athena by Zeta is an accelerant" and that "beta customers plus strong early adoption of Athena" contributed to the quarter’s revenue outperformance (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "After raising the midpoint of our range for 2026 revenue guidance last quarter by $25 million, we are again raising it by $30 million" as the company framed "market share gains" tied to "legacy Marketing Cloud replacement" and vendor consolidation (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "We also saw free cash flow conversion improved to 63%, generating $42 million in free cash flow" and "these results surpassed even our internal stretch goals" at "$26 million or 7 points above the midpoint of our revenue guidance" for the quarter (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Outlook

  • "For the full year 2026, we're increasing the midpoint of our revenue guidance by $30 million to $1.785 billion" and "increasing the midpoint of our 2026 guidance to $397 million" of adjusted EBITDA, plus "increasing our 2026 free cash flow guidance to $235 million" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "For the second quarter, we now expect revenue of $420 million at the midpoint" and "we now expect adjusted EBITDA of $86.6 million at the midpoint" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "None of our guidance increase is related to political candidate revenue" and "we continue to assume" political candidate revenue "will be $15 million in 2026" with "$7 million in the third quarter and $8 million in the fourth quarter"; guidance also assumed "minimal contribution from Athena" and a "conservative view of Marigold, contributing $47.5 million per quarter" for the rest of 2026 (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Financial Results

  • "First quarter revenue of $396 million" and "adjusted EBITDA was $66.1 million at a margin of 16.7%"; management said cost dynamics included "GAAP cost of revenue" at "41%" as "new agency wins" lifted "a higher initial mix of social as a channel" (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "GAAP net loss was $13.2 million" and "GAAP loss per share was $0.06"; management attributed the quarter’s EPS profile to "forecasted costs related to the integration of Marigold" as "the primary driver and not seen as recurring over the rest of the year" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Cash and capital actions included "net cash provided by operating activities was $49.7 million" and "free cash flow of $41.7 million"; the company also said "we repurchased 1.5 million shares for $25.7 million" and had "approximately $138 million remaining" on the authorization (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Q&A

  • David Hynes, Canaccord Genuity: asked if Zeta benefited from competitive issues around The Trade Desk and whether pricing transparency could shift dynamics; CEO David Steinberg responded "the horse is out of the barn" for platforms "that do not have proprietary data" and said Zeta is "taking meaningful market share" and "we are going to continue to beat them handily in the marketplace."
  • David Hynes, Canaccord Genuity: asked what financial indicators will show Athena is "moving the needle"; CFO Christopher Greiner cited "a 7x increase" in "agentic interactions" in Athena’s first week of GA, and said "60% of the AI usage on our platform" was driven by Athena, tying impact to "ARPU expansion" and growth in "multiple use case customers" and "4 or more channels."
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: asked how Zeta changes user behavior to drive Athena adoption; CEO David Steinberg said Athena was available to "100% of our enterprise clients" and that "Our top 30 clients have been onboarded," with "weekly learning and training" and an "ask Athena question of the week" program.
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: asked about inference costs and token usage; CEO David Steinberg said "The vast majority of our queries... are done on our own platforms" and "we are not buying tokens" for customer rollout, while internal tooling "Spade" and "Zippi" supported "75% automated new code creation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were positive and adoption-focused, emphasizing competition, monetization signals, and pipeline velocity, including "what are the signs" Athena is "moving the needle" and whether it can "close new deals quicker" (David Hynes; Terrell Tillman, Truist).
  • Management tone was confident in product differentiation and guidance posture, using phrases such as "Could not be happier with this quarter" and "we are going to continue to beat them handily" while also emphasizing "maintaining our typical conservatism" in guidance (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the prepared tone moved from pre-launch anticipation ("remain on track to make Athena generally available by the end of the first quarter") to post-launch usage proof points ("7x increase" in agentic interactions; "60%" of AI usage driven by Athena) (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised again, shifting from "raising... by $25 million to $1.755 billion" (Q4) to "raising... by $30 million to $1.785 billion" (Q1) (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Athena narrative progressed from "remain on track to make Athena generally available" (Q4) to "Athena is currently available to all of our enterprise customers" with early GA usage metrics and deal anecdotes (CEO David Steinberg).
  • KPIs shifted from Q4 "184 super-scaled" customers and "$1.8 million" super-scaled ARPU to Q1 "189" super-scaled customers and "$1.7 million" super-scaled ARPU, with management highlighting Q1 ARPU growth at "21%" year-over-year (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin pressure risk was tied to channel mix as "new agency wins" drove "a higher initial mix of social," which management said has "a higher cost of revenue"; mitigation relied on agencies scaling into "Zeta-owned channels" over time (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Integration execution risk remained centered on Marigold, with management saying it took "aggressive steps" on synergies expected to benefit margins "in Q2 and into the back half of the year" and describing Q1 integration costs as "not seen as recurring" (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an Athena-driven acceleration in enterprise adoption and sales differentiation, while pairing the quarter’s $396 million revenue and $41.7 million free cash flow with another 2026 guide raise to $1.785 billion in revenue, $397 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $235 million in free cash flow, maintaining that the uplift excluded political candidate revenue and assumed minimal Athena contribution.

data.title: Exponent outlines FY 2026 high single-digit net revenue growth with 27.6%-28.1% EBITDA margin, driven by AI-related demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Exponent (EXPO) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Exponent reported Q1 growth tied to AI-related client work and a mix shift across proactive and reactive engagements. Dr. Catherine Corrigan (President, CEO & Director) said, “Exponent delivered double-digit revenue growth, growth in net revenues and earnings in the first quarter,” adding that growth was “driven by user research studies for consumer electronics clients who are integrating artificial intelligence into their devices,” and by “risk management work for utility clients evaluating asset performance under extreme weather conditions.”

  • Management highlighted reactive demand across disputes and failure analysis. CEO Corrigan said, “Reactive engagements also contributed to our growth with increased dispute-related and failure analysis demand across construction projects, energy facilities and medical devices,” and added, “We saw increased activity from both domestic and international clients related to complex construction challenges and disputes.”

  • Richard Schlenker (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Secretary) framed Q1 execution and capital returns, including a repurchase authorization increase. CFO Schlenker said, “For the first quarter of 2026, total revenues increased 14% to $166.3 million, and revenues before reimbursements... increased 10% to $151.8 million,” and added, “Additionally, our Board approved a $50 million increase in our current stock repurchase program... and reflects our conviction in Exponent's long-term growth trajectory.”

  • Leadership transition was a central governance update. CEO Corrigan said, “John Pye will assume the role of President effective tomorrow, May 1,” and “Eric Anderson will assume the role of Chief Financial Officer, also effective tomorrow, May 1,” while noting, “Rich will continue to play an important role as Executive Vice President, advancing Exponent's strategic priorities while remaining actively engaged with investors.”

Outlook

  • Q2 and FY 2026 guidance remained framed around “high single digits” net revenue growth and stable margin targets. CFO Schlenker said, “For the second quarter... we expect revenues before reimbursements to grow in the high single digits and EBITDA to be 27% to 27.8% of revenues before reimbursements.”

  • For the full year, CFO Schlenker said, “For fiscal year 2026, we are maintaining our revenue and margin guidance,” and specified, “We expect revenues before reimbursements to grow in the high single digits and EBITDA to be 27.6% to 28.1% of revenues before reimbursements.”

  • Operating assumptions included continued hiring and a step-down in Q2 utilization. CFO Schlenker said, “We expect our average technical full-time equivalent employees to increase approximately 5% year-over-year in the second quarter of 2026 and 4% to 5% for the full year 2026,” and “We expect utilization in the second quarter to be 72% to 73%.”

Financial results

  • Q1 EPS and total revenue were disclosed, enabling a comparison to the provided estimates for Q1 FY 2026: $0.59 vs. $0.61 (EPS estimate); $166.3M vs. $153,019,070 (revenue estimate).

  • Profitability and operating metrics were tied to utilization, realized rates, and cost lines. CFO Schlenker said, “EBITDA for the quarter increased 15% to $43.1 million, producing a margin of 28.4% of net revenues,” and added, “Utilization in the first quarter was 76%,” while “The realized rate increase was approximately 4%.”

  • Expense items and below-the-line drivers included share-based award tax impacts, stock-based compensation, infrastructure investment, and interest income. CFO Schlenker said, “During the quarter, we realized a negative tax impact associated with accounting for share-based awards of $900,000,” “Stock-based compensation expense in the quarter was $9.1 million,” “Other operating expenses... were up 6% to $12.8 million due to an investment in our corporate infrastructure,” and “Interest income... decreased to $1.7 million.”

  • Capital allocation emphasized buybacks and dividends alongside continued capex. CFO Schlenker said, “We distributed $16.6 million to shareholders through dividend payments and repurchased $79 million of common stock at an average price of $68.09,” and “During the quarter, capital expenditures were $2.5 million.”

Q&A

  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked how AI, innovation, and energy demand are changing project complexity and competitive positioning; Dr. Catherine Corrigan (President, CEO & Director) pointed to energy modalities “through to wind power, to solar power, even starting to look at things like small modular nuclear reactors,” and John Pye (incoming President) added, “what's interesting and novel now is the physical AI is getting applied to the robotic systems... interacting with [people]... in our warehouses, in our homes.”

  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked why the leadership and governance refresh is happening now; CEO Corrigan said, “The timing is... strategic,” tied to “the increases in the penetration of artificial intelligence into physical systems,” and added, “this evolution of leadership really reflects that in order to accelerate what the company is doing.”

  • Andrew Nicholas, William Blair: Asked about consumer electronics demand broadening and growth conviction; CEO Corrigan described expansion into “health-related products” and “glasses... virtual reality, augmented reality systems,” and said clients are seeking “independent objective advice on what are the best platforms.”

  • Andrew Nicholas, William Blair: Asked whether AI competition is tightening hiring and whether there is a linkage to stock-based compensation; CFO Schlenker said, “it has always been a highly competitive market,” but added, “Our acceptance rate on offers is as high as it's ever been,” and described hiring “150 to 200 new people a year.”

  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Asked about durability of consumer recovery and the pace of improvement; CFO Schlenker said momentum “continued here into the first quarter and are continuing on into the second quarter and beyond,” while also stating, “I can't predict... every quarter.”

  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Asked about Q1 repurchase pace and willingness to stay aggressive; CFO Schlenker said Exponent has “a philosophy... being more aggressive on pullbacks,” and added, “over the last 4 quarters... the company had bought back $177 million of stock, almost 5% of our shares.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: Asked about the large-project portfolio and whether AI changes scope; CFO Schlenker said Exponent does “about 10,000 projects a year,” and CEO Corrigan said AI is increasing complexity, noting that automotive matters now require “trace that decision in the algorithm,” turning “1 test” into “5 tests or 10 tests.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: Asked about chemicals and energy/utilities demand; CEO Corrigan pointed to regulatory complexity (including PFAS) and said, “the drive for more energy... is going to drive disputes,” adding that utilities are also increasing “proactive risk modeling.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive, with multiple congratulations and curiosity-driven questions centered on AI-driven demand durability, talent competition, and the sustainability of buybacks (e.g., Joshua Chan, UBS asked about “the durability of this growth tailwind from consumer”).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and remained confident in Q&A, repeatedly tying demand to AI in physical systems and critical infrastructure; CEO Corrigan said, “we expect demand for Exponent's independent multidisciplinary expertise to continue to grow,” and CFO Schlenker closed, “we... remain confident in the strength of our business.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 maintained the same core narrative around AI-driven complexity and proactive/reactive mix, but added a more explicit “leadership and governance refresh” discussion and more detailed Q&A examples (robotics, data centers, nuclear) as the firm framed the opportunity set.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy and demand drivers were consistent across both calls: Q4 emphasized “diversification,” “user research in consumer electronics,” and “risk management... for utilities,” while Q1 reiterated these and added more concrete examples of AI extending into “battery energy storage systems and data centers” and “small modular nuclear reactors” in Q&A.

  • Guidance language stayed stable: Q4 guided “high single digits” net revenue growth and FY 2026 EBITDA margin of “27.6% to 28.1%,” and Q1 stated, “we are maintaining our revenue and margin guidance,” reiterating the same full-year margin range.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s emphasis on whether consumer electronics was recovering and whether AI could commoditize reactive work to Q1’s heavier emphasis on governance timing, buyback aggressiveness, and how AI is changing project scope (e.g., more testing scenarios and data complexity).

Risks and concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued cyclicality and forecasting limits in consumer-related work. CFO Schlenker said, “I can't predict... what every quarter is going to be.”

  • Hiring remained described as competitive, with mitigation framed around Exponent’s value proposition and recruiting outcomes. CFO Schlenker said, “it has always been a highly competitive market,” while also stating, “Our acceptance rate on offers is as high as it's ever been.”

  • Macro and infrastructure-related complexity was framed as both opportunity and execution burden (more data, more scenarios, and higher consequences of failure), with CEO Corrigan emphasizing that the bar is rising for “edge cases, novel conditions and complex interactions.”

Final takeaway

Exponent’s management emphasized Q1 2026 growth tied to AI-driven demand in consumer user research, utility risk management, and reactive disputes and failure analysis across energy, construction, and medical devices, while keeping FY 2026 guidance unchanged for high single-digit net revenue growth and 27.6% to 28.1% EBITDA margin. The call also marked a leadership transition effective May 1, with John Pye becoming President and Eric Anderson becoming CFO, as the company framed the timing as aligned with AI’s deeper integration into performance-critical physical systems and the resulting increase in project complexity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Huawei expects AI chip sales to surge at least 60% in 2026: report
data.description:
Huawei Technologies expects revenue from its AI chips to ​surge at least 60% ‌this year, driven by strong demand from Chinese companies looking for local alternatives to Nvidia (NVDA), the Financial ​Times reported.

The Chinese ⁠tech giant expects revenue ​from its chips to ​reach about $12B in 2026 based on orders it has already ​received, up from $7.5B ​in 2025, the report added, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

China’s tech groups have placed large orders for Huawei’s latest AI processor Ascend 950PR. The majority of orders this year are for Ascend  ​950PR, ​which ⁠entered mass production in March. The ​company also plans to launch ​an ⁠upgraded version of 950DT in the fourth quarter, the report noted.

Huawei’s AI chip revenue forecast could increase if its manufacturing ramp-up exceeds projections, the report added.

Huawei makes most of its AI chips with Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation. Later this year, two additional fabrication facilities dedicated to its needs are slated to launch, according to the report.

Huawei did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Earlier this week, it was reported that the demand for Huawei's Ascend 950 AI chips had soared after DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) unveiled its V4 AI models that run on Huawei's chips. Major Chinese tech companies, including Alibaba (BABA), ByteDance (BDNCE), and Tencent (TCTZF) (TCEHY), have been reaching ‌out to Huawei about new chip orders.

Huawei has aggressively expanded its chipmaking capabilities as Nvidia (NVDA) continues to face regulatory hurdles in China. Huawei's new AI chip Ascend 950PR outperforms Nvidia's (NVDA) H20 chip — the American company's most powerful chip which was permitted to sell in China until Beijing restricted its import last year. However, 950PR trails Nvidia's H200 AI chips, which have seen regulatory hurdles in China for their sale.

Last week, U.S. Commerce Department Secretary Howard ‌Lutnick said the H200 AI chips have not yet ​been sold to Chinese companies due to difficulties faced by those companies in getting permission from China's government. Beijing is trying to keep the companies' investments focused on ⁠their own domestic industry.

The H200 is the predecessor to Nvidia's current flagship Blackwell chips and the upcoming Vera Rubin. Last month, it was reported that Nvidia (NVDA) won China's ​approval to sell its second-most powerful AI chip, the H200, in the Asian country. In December 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump approved the sale of some advanced AI chips, including the H200 GPUs, to China in exchange for a 25% fee. Trump issued a proclamation in January imposing the 25% tariff on certain high-end chips, including Nvidia's H200 and AMD's (AMD) MI325X.

data.title: Cassiar Gold plans C$5.5M flow-through units offering
data.description:
• Cassiar Gold (CGLCF) on Friday said it plans a non-brokered private placement of 7.27M flow-through units at C$0.76 each to raise about C$5.5M.
  • The proceeds will fund exploration and drilling at its Cassiar Gold Project in northern British Columbia.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about May 21, 2026.
  • Each warrant will be exercisable by the holder to acquire one additional common share at a price of C$0.65 for a period of 22 months following the closing date of the offering.

data.title: NOVONIX sells battery tech unit to former CEO
data.description:
NOVONIX (NVX) on Thursday said it divested its Nova Scotia-based Battery Technology Solutions, or BTS, unit to the division's founder and the company's former CEO, Chris Burns.

The company said the transaction price was $1. The deal includes all assets and liabilities.

BTS will now operate as two independent companies: Avrion Battery Labs and Dryve Battery Materials.

NOVONIX will retain a 15% stake in a new cathode materials business to be called Dryve Battery Materials. The remaining operations will form Avrion Battery Labs, which will offer battery testing systems and research services.

The company said BTS will have about $2M in cash at closing, subject to adjustments. NOVONIX will also provide transition support and allow use of its trademark until the end-2026

BTS was founded by Burns in 2013 and acquired by NOVONIX in 2017.

“By divesting non-core business segments, we are directing our management attention and capital toward advancing domestic supply of this critical mineral and supporting the growth of the North American battery industry,” said CEO Mike O'Kronley.

NVX shares rose over +6% premarket on Friday.

data.title: Rein Therapeutics stock dips after pricing $50M share offering
data.description:
• Rein Therapeutics (RNTX) on Thursday said it priced an underwritten public offering of 50M shares at $1 each, raising $50M in gross proceeds.
  • The company granted underwriters a 45-day option to buy up to 7.5M additional shares to cover overallotments. The offering is expected to close around May 4.
  • The deal includes only common stock and no warrants.
  • Konik Capital Partners is acting as sole book-running manager.
  • Rein said it will use the proceeds to fund a Phase 2 trial of LTI-03 in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and for working capital. It expects the funds, along with existing cash, to support operations into 2028.
  • Shares fell -19.5% premarket on Friday.

data.title: U.S. seeks to deploy hypersonic missile for possible use against Iran - report
data.description:
The U.S. Central Command has requested to deploy the Dark Eagle long-range hypersonic missile to the Gulf region for possible use against Iran, Bloomberg News reported, citing a person with direct knowledge of the matter.

The request noted that Iran moved its launchers out of range of the Precision Strike Missile, which can hit targets at more than 300 miles, justifying the need for the hypersonic missile.

If approved, this would be the first time that the U.S. deployed its hypersonic missile, which hasn't yet been declared fully operational.

The request indicates that the U.S. is preparing for more strikes if the current ceasefire collapses with no deal to end the war. 

The missile component of the Dark Eagle is being developed by Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC), according to the Congressional Research Service. Dynetics, a subsidiary of Leidos (LDOS), is under contract to build the hypersonic glide vehicle.

The Dark Eagle hypersonic missile has faced several delays over the years on account of testing failures and quality issues. To note, Russia and China have already deployed their own versions of the weapon.

data.title: Epsium Enterprise names new CFO
data.description:
• Epsium Enterprise (EPSM) said on Thursday it appointed Ching Wan Wong as CFO effective immediately, succeeding Ming Yin Gordon Au Yeung, who stepped down on April 30.
  • The company stated that Au Yeung’s departure was not due to any disagreement related to operations, policies, or practices.
  • The company said Wong brings more than 20 years of experience spanning financial reporting, internal controls, and regulatory compliance across U.S. and Hong Kong-listed firms.
  • Most recently, he served as CFO and Head of Reporting at Intelligent Living Application Group , where he oversaw SEC compliance and financial disclosures. His background also includes advisory work on Hong Kong Stock Exchange and U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission listing requirements.

data.title: Banner to acquire Pacific Financial in $177M all-stock deal
data.description:
Banner Corporation (BANR) and Pacific Financial Corporation (PFLC) on Friday said companies signed a merger agreement under which Banner will acquire Pacific Financial in an all-stock transaction, expanding its footprint across the Pacific Northwest.

Under the terms of the agreement, Pacific Financial shareholders will receive 0.2633 shares of Banner common stock for each share held. 

Based on Banner’s April 29 closing price of $66.25, the implied deal value stands at $17.44 per share, or approximately $177M in aggregate. Pacific shares closed at $13.48 on Thursday, implying a premium of around 30%.

The banks said the transaction was structured as a tax-free reorganization. Post-merger ownership is to be split with Pacific shareholders holding about 7% of the combined entity and Banner shareholders owning the remaining 93%.

“This transaction expands our presence and density in attractive Western Washington and Western Oregon markets," said Banner CEO Mark Grescovich.

The deal is projected to be immediately accretive to Banner’s 2027 earnings per share, excluding one-time costs.

Pacific Financial was advised by Piper Sandler and Miller Nash, while Banner was advised by BofA Securities and Ballard Spahr.

data.title: Amkor Technology prices $1B convertible debt offering
data.description:
Amkor Technology (AMKR) has priced its private offering of $1B aggregate principal amount of 0.00% convertible senior notes due 2031.

The sale is scheduled to settle on May 5, 2026, subject to customary closing conditions. Amkor also granted the initial purchasers of the notes a 13-day option to purchase up to an additional $150M of notes.

The notes will not bear regular interest, and the principal amount of the notes will not accrete. It will mature on July 15, 2031, unless earlier repurchased, redeemed, or converted.

The initial conversion rate is 9.4013 shares of common stock per $1,000 principal amount of notes, which represents an initial conversion price of around $106.37 per share of common stock - a premium of ~52.5% over the last reported sale price.

Amkor (AMKR) estimates net proceeds of around $981.7M, or ~$1,129M if the initial purchasers fully exercise their option to purchase additional notes. The firm plans to use $49M of the proceeds to fund the cost of entering into the capped call transactions and the remainder for general corporate purposes.
